Level payments can be valued using level annuity factors, decreasing payments using decreasing annuity factors, and payments continuing forever using perpetuity factors. Then discount each deferred stream back to time 0.
answer_text: The present value is approximately \(\boxed{119.40}\), so the correct answer is A: \(119\).

title: Exercise 137
statement: Annual deposits are made into a fund at the beginning of each year for 10 years. The first 5 deposits are 100 each, and deposits increase by 7% per year thereafter. If the fund earns an annual effective rate of 5%, find the accumulated value at the end of 10 years.
solution_steps: The deposits are made at the beginning of each year, so they occur at times \(0,1,2,\ldots,9\).
The first 5 deposits are \(100\), so the deposits at times \(0,1,2,3,4\) are all \(100\).
After that, the deposits increase by \(7\%\) per year: \(100(1.07),100(1.07)^2,\ldots,100(1.07)^5\).
Choose time \(3\) as the valuation date.
At time \(3\), the first four deposits at times \(0,1,2,3\) have accumulated value \(100s_{\overline{4}|0.05}\).

The remaining six deposits at times \(4,5,\ldots,9\) form a geometric annuity-immediate from the perspective of time \(3\), with first payment \(100\), interest rate \(i=0.05\), and growth rate \(r=0.07\).
Thus, their value at time \(3\) is \(100(Ga)_{\overline{6}|0.05,0.07}\).
Therefore, the value at time \(3\) is \(100s_{\overline{4}|0.05}+100(Ga)_{\overline{6}|0.05,0.07}\).
Accumulate this value from time \(3\) to time \(10\): \(AV=1030.35(1.05)^7\).
generalization: When a payment stream is level for a few periods and then grows geometrically, choose a valuation date just before the geometric part begins. Then value the earlier level deposits as an accumulated annuity and the later growing deposits as a geometric annuity-immediate.
answer_text: The accumulated value at the end of 10 years is \(\boxed{1449.81}\).

title: Exercise 138
statement: Stan elects to receive his retirement benefit over 20 years at the rate of 2000 per month, beginning one month from now. The monthly benefit increases by 5% each year. At a nominal rate of 6% convertible monthly, calculate the present value of the retirement benefit.
solution_steps: The nominal annual interest rate is \(6\%\) convertible monthly, so the effective monthly interest rate is \(j=0.06/12=0.005\).
The effective annual interest rate is \(i=(1+j)^{12}-1=(1.005)^{12}-1=0.0616778\).
During the first year, Stan receives \(2000\) at the end of each month, so the accumulated value at the end of the first year is \(2000s_{\overline{12}|j}\).
During the second year, the monthly payment increases by \(5\%\), so the accumulated value at the end of the second year is \(2000(1.05)s_{\overline{12}|j}\).
The rolled-up annual cash flows are \(2000s_{\overline{12}|j},2000(1.05)s_{\overline{12}|j},\ldots,2000(1.05)^{19}s_{\overline{12}|j}\) at times \(1,2,\ldots,20\).
These cash flows form a geometric annuity-immediate with annual effective interest rate \(i\) and annual growth rate \(r=0.05\).
generalization: When payments are monthly within each year but increase annually, first roll up each year's monthly payments to the end of that year using the monthly effective rate. Then value the resulting annual cash flows as a geometric annuity-immediate using the annual effective interest rate and the annual growth rate.
answer_text: The present value of the retirement benefit is \(\boxed{419253.25}\).

title: Exercise 139
statement: Smith receives annual dividends which began one year after an investment of 10000 fifteen years ago. Dividends have increased 2% every year. The dividend Smith received today was 659.74. What is the accumulated value today of Smith's dividends assuming an effective annual interest rate of 5.06%?
solution_steps: Let \(k\) be the first dividend, paid one year after the investment.
Since the investment was made 15 years ago and dividends began one year after the investment, the dividends occur at times \(1,2,\ldots,15\).
The dividends increase by \(2\%\) each year, so the dividend received today at time \(15\) is \(k(1.02)^{14}\).
The accumulated value today is the accumulated value of a geometric annuity-immediate with \(n=15\), \(i=0.0506\), and \(r=0.02\).
generalization: When the last payment of a geometric annuity is given, first backsolve for the first payment using the growth rate. Then use the geometric accumulated value formula to find the value at the final payment date.
answer_text: The accumulated value today is approximately \(\boxed{12270.44}\), so the correct answer is B: At least \(\$12{,}240\), but less than \(\$12{,}300\).

title: Exercise 140
statement: Mary is to receive an annuity with 30 annual payments. The first payment of 1000 is due immediately, and each successive payment is 5% less than the payment for the preceding year. Interest is 12% compounded annually. Determine the present value of the annuity.
solution_steps: The first payment is due immediately, so this is a geometric annuity-due.
There are \(30\) payments, occurring at times \(0,1,2,\ldots,29\).
The annual effective interest rate is \(i=0.12\).
Since each payment is \(5\%\) less than the previous payment, the growth rate is \(r=-0.05\).
generalization: A decreasing geometric annuity can be handled using the same geometric annuity formulas by using a negative growth rate. If each payment is 5% less than the previous payment, then \(r=-0.05\) and \(1+r=0.95\). If the first payment is immediate, use the geometric annuity-due factor.
answer_text: The present value is approximately \(\boxed{6541.04}\), so the correct answer is C: At least \(\$6{,}500\), but less than \(\$6{,}600\).

title: Exercise 141
statement: Bill is due to retire in 20 years, at which time he will start collecting pension benefits. His employer is scheduled to begin funding Bill's pension benefits with the first annual payment due at the end of year 1 and the last annual payment due at the end of year 20. The initial employer's contribution is 2000 and will increase by 3% each year. Employer contributions will earn interest at an annual effective interest rate of 7%. Bill's employer can elect alternative funding: annual contributions beginning at the end of year 6 with the last contribution at the end of year 20. These annual contributions begin at 1000 and increase by Q per year. Under this alternative funding, the employer's contributions will earn an annual effective interest rate of 8%. Determine Q so that both funding options have the same accumulated value at the end of year 20.
solution_steps: Compare the accumulated values at the end of year \(20\).
For the first funding option, the payments are \(2000,2000(1.03),2000(1.03)^2,\ldots,2000(1.03)^{19}\) at times \(1,2,\ldots,20\).
These contributions earn \(7\%\), so the accumulated value at time \(20\) is \(AV_1=2000(Gs)_{\overline{20}|0.07,0.03}\).
For the second funding option, payments are made from time \(6\) through time \(20\), so there are \(15\) payments.
The level part accumulates to time \(20\) as \(1000s_{\overline{15}|0.08}\).
The increasing part accumulates to time \(20\) as \(Q(Is)_{\overline{14}|0.08}\).
generalization: When two funding schedules must have the same accumulated value, value both schedules at the same comparison date. A geometric contribution schedule can be accumulated using the geometric accumulated value formula, while an arithmetic increasing contribution schedule can be decomposed into a level annuity plus an increasing annuity.
answer_text: The required annual increase is \(\boxed{500.50}\), so the correct answer is C: At least \(\$480\), but less than \(\$520\).

title: Exercise 142
statement: Smith will make deposits to a fund each January 1 and July 1 from 1985 through 1995. Each July 1, the level deposit is increased by 10.25% over the previous level. The effective annual interest rate is 10.25%. The fund balance on December 31, 1995 will be 15000. Calculate the amount of Smith's initial deposit on January 1, 1985.
solution_steps: Let \(X\) be Smith's initial deposit on January 1, 1985.
The effective annual interest rate is \(i=0.1025\).
There are 11 January deposits and 11 July deposits.
For the January deposits, the growth rate equals the interest rate, so this is the special case \(r=i\) for a geometric annuity-due.
Thus, \((G\ddot{a})_{\overline{11}|0.1025,0.1025}=11\), and the value of the January deposits is \(11X\).
For the July deposits, the first July deposit is \(1.1025X\), and it occurs one half-year after January 1, 1985.
At July 1, 1985, the value of the July deposits is \(1.1025X(G\ddot{a})_{\overline{11}|0.1025,0.1025}\).
Discounting this value back one half-year gives \(1.1025X(11)(1.1025)^{-1/2}\).
Accumulate this value 11 years to December 31, 1995: \(22.55X(1.1025)^{11}=15000\).
generalization: When the payment growth rate equals the interest rate, use the special case for a geometric annuity-due: \((G\ddot{a})_{\overline{n}|i,i}=n\). If a second payment stream is shifted by a fractional period, value it at its own first payment date and then discount or accumulate by the fractional period.
answer_text: Smith's initial deposit is \(\boxed{227.39}\).

title: Exercise 143
statement: A perpetuity pays 2X one year from today. The annual payments increase by 5% per year thereafter. The effective annual interest rate on this perpetuity is 6%. The present value is 32400. A second perpetuity pays Y one year from now, and the annual payments increase by an amount of X per year thereafter. The effective annual interest rate on this perpetuity is i, and the present value is 24000. A third perpetuity pays Y per year, with the first payment one year from now. The effective annual interest rate on this perpetuity is i, and the present value is 4000. What is Y?
solution_steps: The first perpetuity is a geometric perpetuity-immediate with first payment \(2X\), interest rate \(0.06\), and growth rate \(0.05\).
The third perpetuity is a level perpetuity-immediate with present value \(Ya_{\overline{\infty}|i}=4000\).
Decompose the second perpetuity into a level perpetuity of \(Y\) plus an arithmetic increasing part \(0,X,2X,3X,\ldots\).
The level part has present value \(Ya_{\overline{\infty}|i}\).
The increasing part starts one year later than a standard increasing perpetuity-immediate, so its present value is \(X(Ia)_{\overline{\infty}|i}v_i\).
generalization: When a perpetuity combines a level component and an arithmetic increasing component, decompose the cash flow into a level perpetuity plus an increasing perpetuity. If the increasing part starts one period later than the standard increasing perpetuity-immediate, multiply its value by \(v\).
answer_text: The value of \(Y\) is \(\boxed{360}\).

title: Exercise 144
statement: An annuity provides for 10 annual payments. The first of these payments is 100, and each subsequent payment is 10% higher than the one preceding it. Find the present value, to the nearest 50, of this annuity at the time one year prior to the first payment if i=10%.
solution_steps: The payment growth rate is \(r=0.10\), and the effective interest rate is \(i=0.10\).
Thus, \(r=i\), so we use the special-case formula for a geometric annuity-immediate.
Since the valuation date is one year prior to the first payment, this is a geometric annuity-immediate.
generalization: When the payment growth rate equals the interest rate, each discounted payment in a geometric annuity-immediate has the same value. Therefore, if the first payment is \(P\), the present value is \(Pn v\), where \(n\) is the number of payments and \(v=1/(1+i)\).
answer_text: The present value is approximately \(\boxed{909.09}\), so to the nearest \(50\), the correct answer is C: \(\$900\).

title: Exercise 145
statement: Mary purchases an increasing annuity-immediate for 50000 that makes twenty annual payments as follows: (i) P, 2P, ..., 10P in years 1 through 10; and (ii) 10P(1.05), 10P(1.05)^2, ..., 10P(1.05)^10 in years 11 through 20. The annual effective interest rate is 7% for the first 10 years and 5% thereafter. Calculate P.
solution_steps: Split the annuity into two parts.
For years 1 through 10, the payments are \(P,2P,\ldots,10P\). This is an arithmetic increasing annuity-immediate.
Since the interest rate for the first 10 years is \(7\%\), the present value of the first part is \(P(Ia)_{\overline{10}|0.07}\).
For years 11 through 20, the payments are \(10P(1.05),10P(1.05)^2,\ldots,10P(1.05)^{10}\).
At time 10, this is a geometric annuity-immediate with first payment \(10P(1.05)\), growth rate \(r=0.05\), and interest rate \(i=0.05\).
Thus, the value at time 10 of the second part is \(10P(1.05)(Ga)_{\overline{10}|0.05,0.05}\).
Discount this value back to time 0 at \(7\%\), giving \(100Pv_{0.07}^{10}\).
Therefore, the equation of value is \(50000=P(Ia)_{\overline{10}|0.07}+100Pv_{0.07}^{10}\).
generalization: When an annuity combines an arithmetic increasing part and a deferred geometric part, value each part at the correct interest rate. If the geometric growth rate equals the interest rate, use the special case \((Ga)_{\overline{n}|i,i}=nv_i\). Then discount the deferred value back using the interest rate applicable before the deferral date.
answer_text: The value of \(P\) is approximately \(\boxed{584.29}\), so the correct answer is C: \(584\).

title: Exercise 146
statement: [SOA 11; SOA-IT.014] Mike buys a perpetuity-immediate with varying annual payments. During the first 5 years, the payment is constant and equal to 10. Beginning in year 6, the payments start to increase. For year 6 and all future years, the payment in that year is k% larger than the payment in the year immediately preceding that year, where k < 9.2. At an annual effective interest rate of 9.2%, the perpetuity has a present value of 167.50. Calculate k.
solution_steps: Let \(j\) be the decimal representation of \(k\%\), so \(j=k/100\).
The annual effective interest rate is \(i=0.092\).
The first five payments are level payments of \(10\), so their present value is \(10a_{\overline{5}|0.092}\).
Beginning in year 6, the payments form a geometric perpetuity-immediate.
The first payment of the geometric perpetuity is \(10(1+j)\), and it occurs at time \(6\).
Thus, the value of this geometric perpetuity at time \(5\) is \(10(1+j)/(0.092-j)\).
Discount this value back five years to time \(0\), giving \(10(1+j)(1/(0.092-j))v_{0.092}^5\).
Since \(j\) is the decimal representation of \(k\%\), we get \(k=4.0\).
generalization: For a perpetuity with an initial level period followed by geometric growth, value the level annuity first, then value the deferred geometric perpetuity at the point just before its first payment. Finally, discount the deferred value back to the valuation date.
answer_text: The value of \(k\) is \(\boxed{4.0}\), so the correct answer is A.

title: Exercise 147
statement: Do these on your calculator without pen or paper: given i=0.04, calculate the continuous annuity present value factor for 10 years; given delta=0.06, calculate the continuous annuity present value factor for 20 years; given i=0.10, calculate the continuous annuity accumulated value factor for 15 years; given delta=0.08, calculate the continuous annuity accumulated value factor for 25 years.
generalization: For continuous annuities, the force of interest \(\delta\) replaces the usual denominator \(i\) or \(d\). If the effective rate \(i\) is given, convert it using \(\delta=\ln(1+i)\). If \(\delta\) is given directly, it is often faster to use exponential forms: \(\bar{a}_{\overline{n}|}=\frac{1-e^{-\delta n}}{\delta}\) and \(\bar{s}_{\overline{n}|}=\frac{e^{\delta n}-1}{\delta}\).
answer_text: The answers are \(\bar{a}_{\overline{10}|}=8.272\), \(\bar{a}_{\overline{20}|}=11.647\), \(\bar{s}_{\overline{15}|}=33.336\), and \(\bar{s}_{\overline{25}|}=79.863\).

title: Exercise 148
statement: A continuous perpetuity has a rate of payment of 10 per year for the first 5 years and 20 per year thereafter. If the annual effective rate of interest is 8%, find the present value of this perpetuity.
solution_steps: The annual effective interest rate is \(i=0.08\), so the force of interest is \(\delta=\ln(1.08)\).
For the first 5 years, the perpetuity pays continuously at a rate of \(10\) per year. The present value of this part is \(10\bar{a}_{\overline{5}|}\).
After time \(5\), the perpetuity pays continuously at a rate of \(20\) per year forever. The value at time \(5\) of this continuous perpetuity is \(20\bar{a}_{\overline{\infty}|}\).
Discounting this value back to time \(0\), we get \(20\bar{a}_{\overline{\infty}|}v^5\).

Alternatively, write the cash flow as a continuous perpetuity of \(20\) per year forever minus a continuous temporary annuity of \(10\) per year for the first 5 years: \(PV=20\bar{a}_{\overline{\infty}|}-10\bar{a}_{\overline{5}|}=218.37\).
generalization: For a continuous payment stream with different payment rates over different time intervals, split the stream into separate continuous annuities or perpetuities. A continuous perpetuity paying at rate \(R\) per year has present value \(R/\delta\), and a continuous temporary annuity paying at rate \(R\) for \(n\) years has present value \(R(1-v^n)/\delta\).
answer_text: The present value of the continuous perpetuity is \(\boxed{218.37}\).

title: Exercise 149
statement: The present value of a continuous annuity of 1 per year for a certain number of years is 4. The continuously compounded rate of interest is 12.5%. What is the accumulated value of a continuous annuity of 1 per year for twice that number of years?
solution_steps: Let the unknown number of years be \(n\).
The present value of a continuous annuity of \(1\) per year for \(n\) years is \(\bar{a}_{\overline{n}|}\).
The continuously compounded rate of interest is \(\delta=0.125\).
generalization: When a continuous annuity present value gives information about \(v^n\), use it to infer \((1+i)^n\). Then accumulated values over multiples of \(n\) can often be found without solving explicitly for \(n\).
answer_text: The accumulated value is \(\boxed{24}\).

title: Exercise 150
statement: You are given an effective annual rate of interest i=5%. Which of the following values are greater than or equal to 20? (1) The present value of an annuity of 1 per year payable continuously for 50 years. (2) The accumulated value in 5 years of annual payments starting at 5 and decreasing by 1 per year, with payments starting one year from now. (3) The present value of a perpetuity paying 1 every 5 years and increasing by 1 each payment, with payments starting five years from now.
solution_steps: For value 1, use the continuous annuity formula \(\bar{a}_{\overline{50}|}=\frac{1-v^{50}}{\delta}\).
For value 2, the payments are \(5,4,3,2,1\) at times \(1,2,3,4,5\).
The accumulated value at time 5 is \((Ds)_{\overline{5}|0.05}\).
Using \((Ds)_{\overline{n}|i}=\frac{n-a_{\overline{n}|i}}{i}(1+i)^n\), we get \((Ds)_{\overline{5}|0.05}=17.12\). Since \(17.12<20\), value 2 is not at least 20.
For value 3, the payments are \(1,2,3,\ldots\) at times \(5,10,15,\ldots\).
Let \(j\) be the effective rate per 5 years. Then \(j=(1.05)^5-1=0.27628\).
This is an increasing perpetuity-immediate measured in 5-year periods, so \(PV=(Ia)_{\overline{\infty}|j}\).
Substituting \(j=0.27628\), we get \(PV=16.72\). Since \(16.72<20\), value 3 is not at least 20.
Therefore, none of the three values are greater than or equal to 20.
generalization: When comparing different annuity values, compute each value at the correct valuation date and with the correct payment frequency. For continuous annuities, use \(\delta=\ln(1+i)\). For payments made every several years, convert the annual effective rate into the effective rate per payment period.
answer_text: None of the three values are greater than or equal to 20, so the correct answer is E.

title: Exercise 151
statement: An annuity has the following series of payments: (i) 1000 per year for the first 4 years payable continuously; (ii) 1000 starting at the end of the 5th year, increasing by X per year at the end of years 6 through 11; (iii) 3000 per year at the beginning of years 13 through 15. The nominal rate of interest is 10% compounded twice per year. The accumulated value of the payments at the end of the 15th year is 50000. Determine X.
solution_steps: The nominal rate is \(10\%\) compounded twice per year, so the annual effective rate is \(i=(1+0.10/2)^2-1=0.1025\).
Write an equation of value at time \(0\). Since the accumulated value at time \(15\) is \(50000\), its present value is \(50000v^{15}\).
The first stream is \(1000\) per year payable continuously for the first 4 years, so its present value is \(1000\bar{a}_{\overline{4}|0.1025}\).
The second stream has a level part of \(1000\) paid at the ends of years \(5\) through \(11\). This is \(1000a_{\overline{7}|0.1025}v^4\).
The increasing part is \(X,2X,\ldots,6X\) paid at the ends of years \(6\) through \(11\). This is \(X(Ia)_{\overline{6}|0.1025}v^5\).
The third stream pays \(3000\) at the beginning of years \(13\) through \(15\), which means times \(12,13,14\). Its present value is \(3000a_{\overline{3}|0.1025}v^{11}\).
generalization: For mixed payment streams, write all values at a common valuation date. Continuous payments use continuous annuity factors, level annual payments use ordinary annuity factors, arithmetic increasing payments use increasing annuity factors, and timing words such as beginning of year must be translated carefully into exact time points.
answer_text: The value of \(X\) is approximately \(\boxed{286.89}\), so the correct answer is A: Less than \(\$295\).

title: Exercise 152
statement: Given delta = 0.05, calculate: (1) the present value of a continuously increasing annuity for 10 years, (2) the present value of a continuously decreasing annuity for 10 years, and (3) the present value of a continuously increasing perpetuity.
solution_steps: For the continuously increasing annuity, use \((\bar{I}\bar{a})_{\overline{n}|}=\frac{\bar{a}_{\overline{n}|}-nv^n}{\delta}\).
For the continuously decreasing annuity, use \((\bar{D}\bar{a})_{\overline{n}|}=\frac{n-\bar{a}_{\overline{n}|}}{\delta}\).
For the continuously increasing perpetuity, use \((\bar{I}\bar{a})_{\overline{\infty}|}=\frac{1}{\delta^2}\).
generalization: When the force of interest \(\delta\) is given directly, use \(v^t=e^{-\delta t}\). The main formulas are \((\bar{I}\bar{a})_{\overline{n}|}=\frac{\bar{a}_{\overline{n}|}-nv^n}{\delta}\), \((\bar{D}\bar{a})_{\overline{n}|}=\frac{n-\bar{a}_{\overline{n}|}}{\delta}\), and \((\bar{I}\bar{a})_{\overline{\infty}|}=\frac{1}{\delta^2}\).
answer_text: The answers are \((\bar{I}\bar{a})_{\overline{10}|}=\boxed{36.08}\), \((\bar{D}\bar{a})_{\overline{10}|}=\boxed{42.61}\), and \((\bar{I}\bar{a})_{\overline{\infty}|}=\boxed{400}\).

title: Exercise 153
statement: Payments are made to an account at a continuous rate of 8k + tk, where 0 <= t <= 10. Interest is credited at a force of interest delta_t = 1/(8+t). After 10 years, the account is worth 20000. Calculate k.
solution_steps: The payment rate at time \(t\) is \(f(t)=8k+tk=k(8+t)\).
The accumulation function from time \(0\) to time \(t\) is \(a(t)=\exp\left(\int_0^t \delta_s\,ds\right)\).
The accumulated value at time \(10\) is \(AV=\int_0^{10} f(t)a(t,10)\,dt\), where \(a(t,10)=\frac{a(10)}{a(t)}\).
Substitute into the accumulated value equation: \(20000=\int_0^{10} k(8+t)\left(\frac{18}{8+t}\right)\,dt\).
generalization: For generalized continuous annuities, use the accumulated value formula \(AV=\int_0^n f(t)a(t,n)\,dt\), where \(a(t,n)=a(n)/a(t)\). When the force of interest varies with time, first compute the accumulation function \(a(t)=\exp\left(\int_0^t \delta_s\,ds\right)\).
answer_text: The value of \(k\) is \(\boxed{111.11}\).

title: Exercise 154
statement: You are given: (i) The force of interest at time t is kt^3. (ii) R is the present value of a four-year continuously increasing annuity which has a rate of payment of mt^3 at time t. Calculate R.
solution_steps: The payment rate at time \(t\) is \(f(t)=mt^3\).
The present value of a generalized continuous annuity is \(R=\int_0^4 f(t)\exp\left(-\int_0^t \delta_s\,ds\right)dt\).
generalization: For a generalized continuous annuity, always begin with \(PV=\int f(t)\exp\left(-\int_0^t \delta_s\,ds\right)dt\). If the payment rate resembles the derivative of the accumulated force of interest, a substitution often simplifies the integral immediately.
answer_text: The present value is \(\boxed{\dfrac{m(1-e^{-64k})}{k}}\), so the correct answer is E.

title: Exercise 155
statement: An 11-year annuity has a series of payments 1,2,3,4,5,6,5,4,3,2,1 with the first payment made at the end of the second year. The present value of this annuity is 25 at an interest rate i. A 12-year annuity has a series of payments 1,2,3,4,5,6,6,5,4,3,2,1 with the first payment made at the end of the first year. Calculate the present value of the 12-year annuity at interest rate i.
solution_steps: The 11-year annuity has payments \(1,2,3,4,5,6,5,4,3,2,1\) at times \(2,3,4,\ldots,12\).
This is a deferred pyramid annuity with peak \(6\). A pyramid annuity-immediate with peak \(6\) has present value \(a_{\overline{6}|}\ddot{a}_{\overline{6}|}\).
Since the first payment is deferred one year, the present value of the 11-year annuity is \(v a_{\overline{6}|}\ddot{a}_{\overline{6}|}\).
The 12-year annuity equals the 11-year annuity plus an extra level annuity-immediate of \(1\) paid at times \(1,2,3,4,5,6\).
The present value of this extra stream is \(a_{\overline{6}|}=5\).
Therefore, the present value of the 12-year annuity is \(25+5=30\).
generalization: A pyramid annuity with payments \(1,2,\ldots,n,n-1,\ldots,1\) has present value \(a_{\overline{n}|}\ddot{a}_{\overline{n}|}\). If the pyramid is deferred one period, multiply by \(v\), which can simplify because \(v\ddot{a}_{\overline{n}|}=a_{\overline{n}|}\).
answer_text: The present value of the 12-year annuity is \(\boxed{30}\).

title: Exercise 156
statement: A fund is built with annual payments increasing by 1 from 1 to 10 and then decreasing by 1 to 0. The first payment of 1 is made today. If the fund is used to purchase a 10-year level annuity with the first payment at 20 years from today, what is the amount of the level payment? Assume an annual effective rate of interest of 4%.
solution_steps: The fund payments are \(1,2,3,\ldots,10,9,8,\ldots,2,1\), with the first payment made today.
Thus, the payments occur at times \(0,1,2,\ldots,18\).
This is a pyramid annuity-due with peak \(10\), so its present value at time \(0\) is \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{10}|}^2\).
The 10-year level annuity has its first payment at time \(20\), so its payments occur at times \(20,21,\ldots,29\).
Value both sides at time \(19\), one period before the first level payment.
The accumulated value of the fund at time \(19\) is \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{10}|}^2(1+i)^{19}\).

Since \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{10}|}(1+i)^{10}=\ddot{s}_{\overline{10}|}\) and \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{10}|}(1+i)^9=s_{\overline{10}|}\), the fund value at time \(19\) is \(\ddot{s}_{\overline{10}|}s_{\overline{10}|}\).
Let \(X\) be the level payment. At time \(19\), the value of the level annuity is \(Xa_{\overline{10}|}\).
Therefore, \(X\) is at least \(18\), but less than \(19\).
generalization: A pyramid annuity-due with peak \(n\) has present value \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{n}|}^2\). When the accumulated value is needed at a later time, it can be useful to split the accumulation power so that terms become familiar accumulated annuity factors.
answer_text: The level payment is \(\boxed{18.48}\), so the correct answer is D.

title: Exercise 157
statement: Hilary receives payments of X at the end of each year for n years. The present value of her annuity is 493. Ned receives payments of 3X at the end of each year for 2n years. The present value of his annuity is 2748. Both present values are calculated at the same annual effective interest rate. Determine v^n.
solution_steps: Divide Ned's equation by Hilary's equation: \(\frac{3Xa_{\overline{2n}|}}{Xa_{\overline{n}|}}=\frac{2748}{493}\).
generalization: When two level annuities have terms \(n\) and \(2n\), the ratio trick \(a_{\overline{2n}|}/a_{\overline{n}|}=1+v^n\) can quickly isolate \(v^n\).
answer_text: The value of \(v^n\) is approximately \(\boxed{0.8580}\), so the correct answer is A: \(0.86\).

title: Exercise 158
statement: An annuity pays 1 now, 2 a year from now, 3 two years from now, etc., until a maximum payment of 5 is made, after which time the payments decrease by 1 per year until there are no further payments. Find the present value.
solution_steps: Since the first payment is made now, the payments occur at times \(0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8\).
This is a pyramid annuity-due with peak \(5\).
A pyramid annuity-immediate with peak \(n\) has present value \(a_{\overline{n}|}\ddot{a}_{\overline{n}|}\).
Because the first payment is made immediately, the pyramid is shifted one period earlier, so the present value is \(a_{\overline{5}|}\ddot{a}_{\overline{5}|}(1+i)\).
generalization: A pyramid annuity-immediate with peak \(n\) has present value \(a_{\overline{n}|}\ddot{a}_{\overline{n}|}\). If the first payment is made immediately, the pyramid becomes an annuity-due, and the present value becomes \(\left(\ddot{a}_{\overline{n}|}\right)^2\).
answer_text: The present value is \(\boxed{\left(\ddot{a}_{\overline{5}|}\right)^2}\), so the correct answer is A.

topic: Net Present Value and Internal Rate of Return
title: Exercise 159
statement: A five-year investment project requires an initial investment of 10000 at inception and maintenance expenses at the beginning of each year. The maintenance expense is 300 for the first year and is anticipated to increase 6% each year thereafter. Projected annual returns from the project are 3000 at the end of the first year and decrease by 100 per year thereafter. (a) If the cost of capital is 7%, calculate the NPV of the project. (b) Compute the internal rate of return for the project.
solution_steps: The initial investment and the first maintenance expense occur at time 0, so the cash outflow at time 0 is \(10000+300=10300\).
The maintenance expenses occur at the beginning of each year, so they occur at times \(0,1,2,3,4\).
The returns occur at the ends of years 1 through 5 and are \(3000,2900,2800,2700,2600\).
The IRR is the rate \(i\) that makes the NPV equal to zero.
Using the BA II Plus CF and IRR functions gives \(IRR=0.07234\), or \(7.234\%\).
generalization: For NPV and IRR problems, first build the net cash flow timeline carefully. Expenses at the beginning of each year occur at times 0,1,2,..., while returns at the end of each year occur at times 1,2,3,... . Once the net cash flows are identified, use the NPV function with the given cost of capital and the IRR function to solve for the break-even interest rate.
answer_text: The net present value is \(\boxed{63.71}\), and the internal rate of return is \(\boxed{0.07234}\), or \(\boxed{7.234\%}\).

title: Exercise 160
statement: Jean takes out a loan to be repaid in 120 monthly payments of 3000 each, the first payment being due one month after the date of the loan. The nominal annual interest rate is 6%, convertible monthly. Immediately after making the 60th payment, Jean negotiates to pay the bank a lump sum of 150000 to pay off the loan. Determine the effective annual yield to the lender over the life of the loan.
solution_steps: First compute the original loan amount using the contract interest rate.
The nominal annual interest rate is \(6\%\), convertible monthly, so the monthly rate is \(j=0.06/12=0.005\).
The loan amount is the present value of 120 monthly payments of 3000: \(L=3000a_{\overline{120}|0.005}\).
Now compute the actual yield to the lender based on the payments actually received.
Jean makes 60 monthly payments of 3000 and then immediately after the 60th payment makes a lump sum payment of 150000.
Let \(j^\ast\) be the actual monthly yield to the lender.
The equation of value at time 0 is \(270220.36=3000a_{\overline{60}|j^\ast}+150000(1+j^\ast)^{-60}\).
generalization: The yield to the lender is based on the actual cash flows received, not necessarily the original contract rate. First find the original loan amount using the contractual repayment terms. Then solve for the interest rate that equates this loan amount to the actual payments received by the lender.
answer_text: The effective annual yield is \(\boxed{5.735\%}\), so the correct answer is D: at least \(5.6\%\), but less than \(5.9\%\).

topic: Net Present Value and Internal Rate of Return
title: Exercise 161
statement: [SOA 19; SOA-IT.023] Project P requires an investment of 4000 today. The investment pays 2000 one year from today and 4000 two years from today. Project Q requires an investment of X two years from today. The investment pays 2000 today and 4000 one year from today. The net present values of the two projects are equal at an annual effective interest rate of 10%. Calculate X.
solution_steps: The net present value of Project P is \(NPV_P=-4000+2000v+4000v^2\).
The net present value of Project Q is \(NPV_Q=2000+4000v-Xv^2\).
Since the two net present values are equal, set \(-4000+2000v+4000v^2=2000+4000v-Xv^2\).
generalization: When two projects have equal net present values, write the NPV of each project at the same interest rate and set them equal. Investments are negative cash flows, while payments received are positive cash flows.
answer_text: The value of \(X\) is \(\boxed{5460}\), so the correct answer is D.

title: Exercise 162
statement: Phil invests 10000 now into a fund earning an annual effective rate of 8%, payable annually. The interest payments are reinvested in a separate fund earning an annual effective rate of 6%. Determine the total value of Phil's investment ten years from now.
solution_steps: The original fund earns interest at an annual effective rate of \(8\%\), payable annually.
Therefore, each year, the interest payment is \(10000(0.08)=800\).
The original principal remains in the original fund, so at time \(10\), the original principal contributes \(10000\).
The interest payments of \(800\) occur at times \(1,2,3,\ldots,10\).
These interest payments are reinvested in a separate fund earning \(6\%\).
Thus, the accumulated value of the reinvested interest payments at time \(10\) is \(800s_{\overline{10}|0.06}\).
The total value at time \(10\) is therefore \(10000+800s_{\overline{10}|0.06}\).
generalization: When interest payments are paid out and reinvested in a separate fund, separate the original principal from the reinvested interest. The principal remains in the original fund, while the interest payments accumulate at the reinvestment rate.
answer_text: The total value of Phil's investment ten years from now is \(\boxed{20544.64}\).

title: Exercise 163
statement: Tiger invests 1000 at the beginning of each year into a fund earning a nominal annual rate of 8% convertible semiannually, payable semiannually. The interest payments are reinvested in a separate fund earning an annual effective rate of 6%. Determine the total value of Tiger's investment ten years from now.
solution_steps: Tiger deposits \(1000\) at the beginning of each year for 10 years, so the deposits occur at times \(0,1,2,\ldots,9\).
Since interest is paid out and reinvested separately, the original principal remains in the original fund.
Therefore, the principal balance at time \(10\) is \(10(1000)=10000\).
The nominal annual rate is \(8\%\) convertible semiannually, so the semiannual interest rate is \(0.08/2=0.04\).
Each deposit of \(1000\) earns a semiannual interest payment of \(1000(0.04)=40\).
The interest payments are reinvested in a separate fund earning an annual effective rate of \(6\%\).
Let \(j\) be the effective semiannual reinvestment rate. Then \(j=(1.06)^{1/2}-1\).
During each year, each active deposit produces two semiannual interest payments of \(40\). Their accumulated value at the end of that year is \(40s_{\overline{2}|j}\).
At the end of years \(1,2,\ldots,10\), the number of active deposits is \(1,2,\ldots,10\).
Therefore, the accumulated value of the reinvested interest payments at time \(10\) is \(40s_{\overline{2}|j}(Is)_{\overline{10}|0.06}\).
The total value at time \(10\) is \(10(1000)+40s_{\overline{2}|j}(Is)_{\overline{10}|0.06}\).
generalization: When interest is paid periodically and reinvested in a separate fund, separate the original principal from the reinvested interest. If interest is paid semiannually but the reinvestment rate is annual effective, convert the reinvestment rate to the corresponding semiannual effective rate before valuing the semiannual interest payments.
answer_text: The total value of Tiger's investment ten years from now is \(\boxed{15373.80}\).

title: Exercise 164
statement: At time t=0, Rory invests 2000 in a fund earning 8% convertible quarterly, but payable annually. He reinvests each interest payment in individual separate funds each earning 9% convertible quarterly, but payable annually. The interest payments from the separate funds are accumulated in a side fund that earns an annual effective rate of 7%. Determine the total value of all funds at t=10.
solution_steps: Convert the nominal annual rate of 8% convertible quarterly into an annual effective rate: \(i_1=\left(1+\frac{0.08}{4}\right)^4-1=0.08243\).
The original fund pays interest annually, so the annual interest payment from the original fund is \(2000(0.08243)=164.86\).
These interest payments are made at times \(1,2,\ldots,10\).
Convert the nominal annual rate of 9% convertible quarterly into an annual effective rate: \(i_2=\left(1+\frac{0.09}{4}\right)^4-1=0.09308\).
Each reinvested interest payment of \(164.86\) earns annual interest of \(164.86(0.09308)=15.35\).
At time \(10\), the original fund still contains the original principal \(2000\).
The ten separate 9% funds contain the ten reinvested interest payments, so they contribute \(10(164.86)\).
The side fund accumulates the interest payments from the separate funds at 7%. These payments form an increasing accumulated annuity with payments \(15.35,2(15.35),\ldots,9(15.35)\).
Thus, the accumulated value in the side fund at time \(10\) is \(15.35(Is)_{\overline{9}|0.07}\).
generalization: For multi-layer reinvestment problems, separate each fund: original principal, reinvested interest principal, and interest generated by the reinvested funds. Then accumulate each layer at the rate that applies to that layer.
answer_text: The total value of all funds at \(t=10\) is \(\boxed{4485.49}\).

title: Exercise 165
statement: Mary invests 1000 at the end of each year for 5 years at an annual effective rate of 9% and reinvests the interest at an annual effective rate of 9%. At the end of 5 years, her investment has a value of X. John invests 1000 at the beginning of each year for 5 years at an annual effective rate of 10% and reinvests the interest at an annual effective rate of 8%. At the end of 5 years, his investment has a value of Y. Calculate Y - X.
solution_steps: Mary invests \(1000\) at the end of each year for 5 years.
Since Mary's investment rate and reinvestment rate are both \(9\%\), her value is equivalent to an ordinary accumulated annuity-immediate.
John invests \(1000\) at the beginning of each year for 5 years.
The original principal remains in the original fund, so it contributes \(5(1000)=5000\) at time 5.
Each deposit earns annual interest of \(1000(0.10)=100\).
Because the deposits are made at the beginning of each year, the annual interest payments form the increasing pattern \(100,200,300,400,500\).
These interest payments are reinvested at \(8\%\).
generalization: When the reinvestment rate is different from the original investment rate, separate the principal from the reinvested interest. If deposits are made at the beginning of each year, the number of deposits earning interest grows over time, often creating an increasing annuity pattern for the reinvested interest payments.
answer_text: The value of \(Y-X\) is approximately \(\boxed{685.20}\), so the correct answer is E: \(685\).

title: Exercise 166
statement: Payments of 1000 are invested at the end of each year for 5 years. The payments earn interest at an annual effective rate of 10%. The interest can be reinvested at an annual effective rate of 6% in the first 4 years and at an annual effective rate of k thereafter. The amount in the fund at the end of 5 years is 6090. Calculate k.
solution_steps: The payments of \(1000\) are made at the end of each year for 5 years, so the deposits occur at times \(1,2,3,4,5\).
Since interest is paid out and reinvested separately, the original principal at time \(5\) is \(5(1000)=5000\).
Therefore, the reinvested interest must account for \(6090-5000=1090\).
Each deposit earns annual interest of \(1000(0.10)=100\).
The interest payments received at times \(2,3,4,5\) are \(100,200,300,400\).
The first three interest payments \(100,200,300\) are accumulated to time \(4\) at \(6\%\), giving \(100(Is)_{\overline{3}|0.06}\).
This amount is then accumulated from time \(4\) to time \(5\) at rate \(k\), giving \(100(Is)_{\overline{3}|0.06}(1+k)\).
The final interest payment of \(400\) is received at time \(5\), so it is already at the valuation date.
Thus, the equation of value at time \(5\) is \(6090=5000+100(Is)_{\overline{3}|0.06}(1+k)+400\).
generalization: For reinvestment problems, separate the original principal from the interest payments. If the reinvestment rate changes after a certain time, accumulate the interest payments up to the change date using the first rate, then accumulate from the change date to the valuation date using the new rate.
answer_text: The value of \(k\) is approximately \(\boxed{10.5\%}\), so the correct answer is D.

title: Exercise 167
statement: Amy invests 1000 at an effective annual rate of 14% for 10 years. Interest is payable annually and is reinvested at an annual effective rate of i. At the end of 10 years, the accumulated interest is 2341.08. Bob invests 150 at the end of each year for 20 years at an annual effective rate of 15%. Interest is payable annually and is reinvested at an annual effective rate of i. Find Bob's accumulated interest at the end of 20 years.
solution_steps: First use Amy's investment to determine the reinvestment rate \(i\).
Amy invests \(1000\) at an effective annual rate of \(14\%\), so her annual interest payment is \(1000(0.14)=140\).
These interest payments are reinvested at annual effective rate \(i\).
The accumulated value of Amy's reinvested interest at the end of 10 years is \(140s_{\overline{10}|i}\).
Using a financial calculator with \(N=10\), \(PV=0\), \(PMT=-140\), and \(FV=2341.08\), we get \(I/Y=11\).
Bob invests \(150\) at the end of each year for 20 years.
Each deposit earns annual interest of \(150(0.15)=22.5\).
Since the first deposit is made at time 1, the interest payments occur from time 2 through time 20.
The accumulated interest payments form an increasing annuity with 19 payments: \(22.5,2(22.5),3(22.5),\ldots,19(22.5)\).
Therefore, Bob's accumulated interest at the end of 20 years is \(22.5(Is)_{\overline{19}|0.11}\).
generalization: When interest is payable annually and reinvested separately, accumulated interest should be valued separately from principal. Use the first investor's information to solve for the reinvestment rate, then apply that same reinvestment rate to the second investor's interest-payment stream.
answer_text: Bob's accumulated interest is approximately \(\boxed{9041.49}\), so the closest answer is E: \(9040\).

title: Exercise 168
statement: Sally lends 10000 to Tim. Tim agrees to pay back the loan over 5 years with monthly payments payable at the end of each month. Sally can reinvest the monthly payments from Tim in a savings account paying interest at 6%, compounded monthly. The yield rate earned on Sally's investment over the five-year period turned out to be 7.45%, compounded semiannually. What nominal rate of interest, compounded monthly, did Sally charge Tim on the loan?
solution_steps: Sally can reinvest Tim's monthly payments at a nominal annual rate of \(6\%\), compounded monthly, so the monthly reinvestment rate is \(0.06/12=0.005\).
Sally's yield rate is \(7.45\%\), compounded semiannually, so the semiannual yield rate is \(0.0745/2\).
Over 5 years, there are \(10\) semiannual periods.
Using \(\text{what I have}=\text{what I demand}\), we write \(Xs_{\overline{60}|0.005}=10000\left(1+\frac{0.0745}{2}\right)^{10}\).
Now let \(j\) be the monthly interest rate charged on Tim's loan.
The nominal annual rate compounded monthly is \(12j\).
generalization: When the reinvestment rate differs from the yield rate, first use the yield condition to determine the payment amount by equating the accumulated value of reinvested payments to the accumulated value required by the desired yield. Then use that payment amount to solve for the loan interest rate.
answer_text: The nominal annual rate of interest charged on the loan is \(\boxed{8.8\%}\), compounded monthly.

title: Exercise 169
statement: An investor purchased a 5-year financial instrument. The investor receives payments of 1000 at the end of each year for 5 years. These payments earn interest at an effective rate of 4% per year. At the end of the year, this interest is reinvested at the rate of 3% per year. Calculate the purchase price to the investor to produce a yield rate of 4%.
solution_steps: The investor receives payments of \(1000\) at times \(1,2,3,4,5\).
At time \(5\), the original payments contribute \(5(1000)=5000\).
Each payment of \(1000\) earns annual interest of \(1000(0.04)=40\).
The interest payments are reinvested at \(3\%\).
The reinvested interest payments form an increasing annuity: \(40,2(40),3(40),4(40)\).
Therefore, the accumulated value of the reinvested interest at time \(5\) is \(40(Is)_{\overline{4}|0.03}\).
Thus, the total accumulated value at time \(5\) is \(5(1000)+40(Is)_{\overline{4}|0.03}\).
Since the investor wants a yield rate of \(4\%\), the purchase price must accumulate to \(P(1.04)^5\).
Using \(\text{what I have}=\text{what I demand}\), write \(5(1000)+40(Is)_{\overline{4}|0.03}=P(1.04)^5\).
generalization: When the reinvestment rate differs from the yield rate, accumulate the cash inflows using the reinvestment rate and equate that accumulated value to the purchase price accumulated at the desired yield rate.
answer_text: The purchase price is \(\boxed{4448.42}\).

title: Exercise 170
statement: Esther invests 100 at the end of each year for 12 years at an annual effective interest rate of i. The interest payments are reinvested at an annual effective rate of 5%. The accumulated value at the end of 12 years is 1748.40. Calculate i.
solution_steps: Esther deposits \(100\) at the end of each year for 12 years, so the deposits occur at times \(1,2,\ldots,12\).
Since interest payments are paid out and reinvested separately, the original principal at time \(12\) is \(100(12)=1200\).
Each deposit earns annual interest of \(100i\).
The interest payments form an increasing annuity because more deposits are earning interest as time passes.

At time \(2\), there is one interest payment of \(100i\). At time \(3\), there are two interest payments of \(100i\), and this continues until time \(12\), when there are eleven interest payments of \(100i\).
Therefore, the accumulated value of the reinvested interest at time \(12\) is \(100i(Is)_{\overline{11}|0.05}\).
generalization: When deposits earn interest that is paid out and reinvested separately, separate the original principal from the reinvested interest. If deposits are made at the end of each year, the interest payments often form an increasing annuity because the number of deposits earning interest increases over time.
answer_text: The annual effective interest rate is \(\boxed{7\%}\), so the correct answer is B.

title: Exercise 171
statement: An investor pays P for an annuity which provides payments of 100 at the beginning of each month for 10 years. These payments are invested at a nominal annual interest rate of 12% convertible monthly. Monthly interest payments are reinvested at a nominal annual interest rate of 6% convertible monthly. The annual yield rate over the 10-year period is 8% effective. Calculate P.
solution_steps: The payments are invested at a nominal annual interest rate of \(12\%\) convertible monthly, so the monthly interest rate is \(0.12/12=0.01\).
Each payment of \(100\) earns monthly interest of \(100(0.01)=1\).
The monthly interest payments are reinvested at a nominal annual interest rate of \(6\%\) convertible monthly, so the monthly reinvestment rate is \(0.06/12=0.005\).
At the end of 10 years, the original 120 payments contribute \(100(120)=12000\).
The reinvested interest payments form an increasing annuity: \(1,2,3,\ldots,120\).
Thus, the accumulated value of the reinvested interest is \((Is)_{\overline{120}|0.005}\).
Therefore, the total accumulated value is \(100(120)+(Is)_{\overline{120}|0.005}\).
The investor wants an annual effective yield rate of \(8\%\), so the purchase price \(P\) must accumulate to \(P(1.08)^{10}\).
Using \(\text{what I have}=\text{what I demand}\), write \(P(1.08)^{10}=100(120)+(Is)_{\overline{120}|0.005}\).
generalization: When cash flows are invested in one fund and the interest payments are reinvested in a separate fund, separate the original payments from the reinvested interest. Then use the yield condition by equating the accumulated value of what the investor has to the purchase price accumulated at the desired yield rate.
answer_text: The purchase price is approximately \(\boxed{9699.16}\), so the closest answer is C: \(9700\).

title: Exercise 172
statement: Frances borrows 10000 and agrees to make 20 equal payments where the first payment is due in one year. Each year, she will pay interest at 12% effective on the outstanding principal. The lender wishes to sell the loan to an investor immediately after the loan is made. Determine the sale price such that the investor will achieve a yield of 15% on this investment.
solution_steps: The loan is repaid with 20 equal annual payments, and the loan interest rate is \(12\%\) effective.
Now look at the investor's cash flows.
At time \(0\), the investor pays the sale price \(P\).
At times \(1,2,\ldots,20\), the investor receives annual payments of \(1338.79\).
The investor wants a yield rate of \(15\%\). Therefore, the sale price is the present value of these 20 payments at \(15\%\).
Therefore, the sale price is less than \(8400\).
generalization: When a loan is sold immediately after it is made, first determine the borrower's scheduled payments using the original loan rate. Then price the loan from the investor's perspective by discounting those payments at the investor's desired yield rate.
answer_text: The sale price is \(\boxed{8379.93}\), so the correct answer is A: less than \(8400\).

title: Exercise 173
statement: A loan is repaid with 20 annual payments of 1000, with the first payment one year from now. The interest rate is 6%. Find the loan balance after the 15th payment using: (a) the prospective method; (b) the retrospective method; (c) the BA II Plus AMORT worksheet.
solution_steps: The loan has 20 annual payments of 1000 and an annual effective interest rate of 6%.
We want the outstanding loan balance immediately after the 15th payment, denoted \(OB_{15}\).
Using the prospective method, after the 15th payment there are \(20-15=5\) payments remaining.
Using the retrospective method, first find the original loan amount: \(L=1000a_{\overline{20}|0.06}=11469.92\).
At time 15, the accumulated value of the original loan is \(11469.92(1.06)^{15}\).
The accumulated value of the first 15 payments is \(1000s_{\overline{15}|0.06}\).
Using the BA II Plus, enter \(N=20\), \(I/Y=6\), \(PMT=1000\), and \(FV=0\), then compute \(PV=-11469.92122\).
In the AMORT worksheet, set \(P1=15\) and \(P2=15\).
The calculator gives \(BAL=-4212.363786\), so the outstanding loan balance is \(4212.36\).
generalization: The prospective method finds the outstanding loan balance as the present value of the remaining payments. The retrospective method finds the balance as the accumulated value of the original loan minus the accumulated value of the payments already made. Both methods give the same outstanding balance.
answer_text: The outstanding loan balance after the 15th payment is \(\boxed{4212.36}\).

title: Exercise 174
statement: A loan of 4724.80 is repaid in annual payments of 1000, 1500, and 3000, with the first payment one year from now. The interest rate is 5% for year 1, 10% for year 2, and 5% for year 3. Build the amortization schedule for this loan.
correct: The amortization schedule has balances 4724.80, 3961.04, 2857.14, and 0.00.
solution_steps: For year 1, the interest rate is \(5\%\), so \(I_1=4724.80(0.05)=236.24\).
The first payment is \(1000\), so the principal paid is \(P_1=1000-236.24=763.76\).
The outstanding balance after the first payment is \(OB_1=4724.80-763.76=3961.04\).
For year 2, the interest rate is \(10\%\), so \(I_2=3961.04(0.10)=396.10\).
The second payment is \(1500\), so the principal paid is \(P_2=1500-396.10=1103.90\).
The outstanding balance after the second payment is \(OB_2=3961.04-1103.90=2857.14\).
For year 3, the interest rate is \(5\%\), so \(I_3=2857.14(0.05)=142.86\).
The third payment is \(3000\), so the principal paid is \(P_3=3000-142.86=2857.14\).
The outstanding balance after the third payment is \(OB_3=2857.14-2857.14=0.00\).

Thus, the amortization schedule is: time 0 balance \(4724.80\); time 1 payment \(1000\), interest \(236.24\), principal \(763.76\), balance \(3961.04\); time 2 payment \(1500\), interest \(396.10\), principal \(1103.90\), balance \(2857.14\); time 3 payment \(3000\), interest \(142.86\), principal \(2857.14\), balance \(0.00\).
generalization: When interest rates vary by year, use the applicable rate for each period. The interest paid in year \(t\) is \(I_t=OB_{t-1}i_t\), the principal paid is \(P_t=R_t-I_t\), and the new outstanding balance is \(OB_t=OB_{t-1}-P_t\).
answer_text: The loan is fully amortized after the third payment. The final outstanding balance is \(\boxed{0.00}\).

title: Exercise 175
statement: On December 31, 1984, Smith borrowed 5000 to be repaid in four years with level payments made at the end of every quarter. The first payment was made on March 31, 1985. The effective annual interest rate was 4%. What was the amount of interest paid in 1986?
solution_steps: The loan is repaid over 4 years with quarterly payments, so the number of payments is \(4(4)=16\).
The effective annual interest rate is \(4\%\), so the effective quarterly interest rate is \(j=(1.04)^{1/4}-1\).
Let \(R\) be the quarterly payment. The loan equation is \(5000=Ra_{\overline{16}|j}\).
The first four payments are made in 1985, so payments 1 through 4 correspond to 1985.
The next four payments are made in 1986, so payments 5 through 8 correspond to 1986.
Using the BA II Plus AMORT worksheet, enter \(N=16\), \(I/Y=100((1.04)^{1/4}-1)\), \(PV=-5000\), and \(FV=0\).
Then use \(P1=5\) and \(P2=8\) in the AMORT worksheet.
Therefore, the amount of interest paid in 1986 is \(132.71\).
generalization: For amortization problems asking for total interest paid over a calendar year, identify which payment numbers fall in that year. Then use the AMORT worksheet with \(P1\) equal to the first relevant payment number and \(P2\) equal to the last relevant payment number.
answer_text: The amount of interest paid in 1986 is \(\boxed{132.71}\).
